content.full_text:
State of California 
Memorandum 
To 
File 
Date 
:April 7, 1977 
From 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Ken Finney 
Subject: 
Telephone conversation with Terry Andrus, Orange County 
County Counsel's Office. 
Terry Andrus, an attorney in the Orange County County 
Counsel's Office, called today to inquire about the report-
ing obligations incurred by a judge under the following 
circumstances. 
The judge was invited by an entertainer to 
spend a few days in Las Vegas. 
The entertainer is given 
a suite of rooms by a hotel while he is in Las Vegas and 
he allowed the judge to use some of the rooms. 
In addition, 
the judge received food and beverages free of charge while 
he was in Las Vegas. 
Some time after receiving this gift 
but prior to the closing date for the period covered by the 
judge's Statement of Economic Interests, the judge picked 
up the tab for a dinner that he had with the entertainer. 
Also, the judge gave the entertainer a cartoon that he was 
able to obtain from the cartoonist who had drawn it. 
The 
cartoon had little value to the cartoonist or to the judge 
but because the entertainer was caricatured in the cartoon, 
it had great value to the entertainer. 
Apparently, the 
judge was able to obtain the original and to give it to the 
entertainer. 
The judge asked the County Counsel whether under the foregoing 
circumstances he had provided sufficient reciprocity to negate 
receipt of a gift by reason of the time he spent in Las Vegas. 
I advised the County Counsel that I did not think this was the 
case. 
I said that the gift received amounted to the food and 
beverage and the value of staying in a suite of rooms in Las 
Vegas. 
The judge had wondered whether the suite of rooms had 
to be valued at what another person would pay to stay in the 
suite since they were, in effect, the residence of the enter-
tainer while the entertainer was in Las Vegas. 
I stated to 
the County Counsel that I did not think this standard made 
sense and that the fair market value of the rooms would be 
what another person would have to pay to stay there. 
I also informed the County Counsel that I did not think the 
one dinner tab and the cartoon would suffice as reciprocity. 
Although the cartoon might have great value to the entertainer, 
it apparently had very little value in terms of its marketability. 


File 
April 7, 1977 
2 
Again, I said the question would be the fair market value of 
the cartoon and since that apparently was low, it obviously 
would not suffice to offset the value of a few days in Las 
Vegas. 
Accordingly, I informed the County Counsel that the judge 
should report the gift of the room in Las Vegas. 
On the 
other hand, I also stated that if the judge were so inclined, 
he could seek an opinion from the Commission on this issue. 
The County Counsel responded that he would inform the judge 
of this but that he would probably try to persuade him to 
amend his Statement of Economic Interests and disclose the 
gift. 
KF:jo

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a judge who received complimentary hotel accommodations and meals in Las Vegas from an entertainer has satisfied gift reporting obligations through subsequent reciprocal gifts (a dinner and a cartoon), and how to value these items under Political Reform Act reporting requirements.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The judge must report the gift of Las Vegas hotel accommodations and meals because the subsequent reciprocal gifts (one dinner and a cartoon of minimal fair market value) do not constitute sufficient reciprocity to negate the reporting obligation. Fair market value standards apply to both the hotel suite and cartoon, and neither the dinner nor cartoon offset the value of several days of lodging and meals.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Internal memorandum documenting telephonic advice rather than formal written response. No traditional QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections; extracted question and conclusion from narrative description of the inquiry and staff's stated advice. Document reflects preliminary staff guidance with suggestion that judge could seek formal Commission opinion if desired.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a judge who received complimentary hotel accommodations and meals in Las Vegas from an entertainer has satisfied gift reporting obligations through subsequent reciprocal gifts (a dinner and a cartoon), and how to value these items under Political Reform Act reporting requirements.

CONCLUSION: The judge must report the gift of Las Vegas hotel accommodations and meals because the subsequent reciprocal gifts (one dinner and a cartoon of minimal fair market value) do not constitute sufficient reciprocity to negate the reporting obligation. Fair market value standards apply to both the hotel suite and cartoon, and neither the dinner nor cartoon offset the value of several days of lodging and meals.

The judge was invited by an entertainer to spend a few days in Las Vegas. embedding.summary: FPPC staff advised Orange County counsel regarding gift reporting obligations for a judge who received complimentary Las Vegas accommodations and meals from an entertainer. The staff concluded the judge must report these gifts despite providing reciprocal gifts (a dinner and cartoon) because they lack sufficient fair market value to offset the original gift.
